nativeRange.habitat: Forest streams and tributaries with clear water, sand, and rock or pebble substrates. Recorded from river systems in Sumatra (Indragiri, Batang Hari, Musi drainages) and Borneo.

diet.notes: Readily accepts flakes, small pellets, freeze-dried, and frozen foods. Has been documented eating algae, small invertebrates, plant matter, worms, daphnia, and bloodworms. Benefits from vegetable roughage such as blanched zucchini or cucumber.

summary: The Tiger Barb is a lively, active schooling fish with a well-documented reputation for nipping the fins of slower or long-finned tankmates. Groups of 8 or more tend to distribute aggression more evenly, making them better suited to a community with faster, similarly-sized fish.
careNotes:
The Tiger Barb has been reclassified several times through the history of aquarium keeping — passing through the genera Barbus, Capoeta, and Systomus — before being placed in the genus Puntigrus following a 2013 revision. The scientific name Puntigrus tetrazona is current as of 2026, though older names still appear frequently on livestock labels and in older literature.

Fin-nipping tendency is severe and well-documented. Long-finned species such as bettas, angelfish, fancy guppies, and long-fin danios, along with slow or timid tankmates, tend to suffer significant fin damage and stress when kept with Tiger Barbs. Groups of 8–12 individuals generally distribute aggression within the school more evenly and reduce targeted nipping at other species, though the behavior is never fully eliminated.

Several selectively bred color variants are available in the hobby: the Green Tiger Barb (also called the Moss Barb, a green-black morph), the Albino Tiger Barb (pale pinkish-gold with red fins), and Golden, Platinum, and Blushing strains. All belong to the same species (Puntigrus tetrazona) and share identical care requirements. Fluorescent GloFish Tiger Barb lines are also commercially sold.

Wild-caught specimens are rarely traded; most aquarium stock is captive-bred and in many cases commercially farmed at scale. Artificially dye-injected specimens, sometimes sold as 'painted' or 'tattooed' barbs, are occasionally encountered — these practices are widely regarded as harmful to the fish.

A school of 6 is the accepted minimum, but 8–12 individuals is strongly advisable for behavioral stability.
breedingNotes: The Tiger Barb is an egg-scattering free spawner that provides no parental care. Females tend to be larger and rounder-bodied than males; males typically show brighter red coloration on the nose and fin edges. Spawning is generally triggered by moving a well-conditioned pair or small group into a separate, shallow spawning tank set up with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mesh across the bottom — the mesh or plants help protect the eggs from being eaten by the adults immediately after spawning. Eggs are adhesive and typically hatch within 36–48 hours at 26°C, with fry becoming free-swimming within a few days. Newly hatched fry require infusoria or fine commercial fry foods during their first days. Breeding is generally considered moderate in difficulty: the fish tend to spawn readily, but preventing egg-eating and raising fry to a healthy size both require active management.
